summary: electron-orbit ships an obfuscated import-time TLS beacon to attacker-controlled Azure Blob endpoint
details: On require/import, dist/index.js invokes setupAutoRenderOnImport() which, in Node contexts (no document), calls prefetchRegisteredIcons() → retrieveIcon() against a hardcoded URL whose hostname electronorbit.blob.core.windows.net, path, HTTP verb, headers, and even the 'net' module name are stored as XOR-encoded byte arrays decoded by a __s() helper. Instead of using node:https, the package ships a hand-rolled TLS 1.3 ClientHello/handshake/AEAD record-layer implementation in src/aetherls.ts and opens raw TCP sockets directly, bypassing standard proxy interception and TLS inspection. Activation is gated on sha256(process.env.BuildType) matching a hardcoded hash, so the beacon only fires in attacker-known environments. The README documents only Electron runtime/launch-plan APIs and never mentions icon fetching or Azure blob endpoints — the 'icon' surface is a cover story not present in the documented API. The package name is also a word-swap of the legitimate orbitdb-archive/orbit-electron project and falsely claims that author identity. Separately, postinstall (install.js) collects hostname, username, platform, arch, and nodeVersion to local disk and defines an unused XOR decoder identical to the one used by the import-time beacon — staging consistent with the same operator. The combination of hand-rolled TLS to evade inspection, XOR-obfuscated destination/module names, env-hash activation gating, cover-story API framing, and project-name impersonation is purpose-built covert C2/staging infrastructure.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
